setting.note: When Americans arrive to drill for oil, the timeless desert oasis of Wadi al-Uyoun is bulldozed away and its people scattered, many washing up in the raw coastal boomtown of Harran, remade by the oil economy into a place of camps, wages and grievance. Munif's great banned epic of the Arabian oil age is drawn from the real eastern province of Saudi Arabia: the vanished oasis of the interior, the coming of the machines, and the segregated oil town on the Gulf where a new and bitter world is born.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Abdelrahman Munif, first published 1984 (Mudun al-Milh). Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Cities of Salt, Abdelrahman Munif (1984). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The burnt earth-red of the razed oasis and the oil-scorched desert, the colour of a world torn up by its roots. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
